# thatsRekt

> An on-chain registry of hack alerts for EVM blockchains, run as a public good. Whitelisted security teams post structured alerts the moment they spot a live exploit — attacker addresses, victim contracts, free-form context. Other whitelisters race to vouch (`confirm`) or refute (`disconfirm`) on-chain. Wallets, DEXes, bridges, and lending markets read the registry directly to protect their users in real time.

The registry is the canonical source. The web app and Telegram channel are convenience surfaces over the same on-chain data — anyone running an indexer can build their own.

## Architecture

- **Smart contracts.** Single proxy per chain, deployed via CREATE2 at the same address across chains when initialized with the same `(governance, whitelist)` triple. Posters call `post(title, attackers[], victims[], note, attackedAt)` and emit `PostCreated`. Confirmations are first-class: `confirm(postId, direction)` and `unconfirm(postId)` push integers up/down per post. Three-role governance (Owner / WhitelistAdmin / WhitelistRemover) with asymmetric timelocks — adds are 3-day, instant kill-switch on removes.
- **Indexer.** Subsquid processor per chain, mirrored Postgres schema across chains. A Mesh (graphql-tools) gateway stitches per-chain squids into a unified `posts(limit, offset, chains)` query.
- **Web app.** Vite + React SPA pulling from the unified GraphQL endpoint. Posts are rendered on a feed with chain badges, attacker/victim counts, and an on-chain confirmation score. Per-post detail pages have rich Markdown rendering of the `note` body.
- **Telegram bot.** A 256MB Fargate Go service polls the GraphQL endpoint every 10 seconds and broadcasts new posts to `@thatsrekt_alerts`. Inline ✓/✗ vote buttons are cosmetic — on-chain confirmations come from whitelisted addresses calling `confirm(...)` directly.

## Key URLs

- Live feed: https://thatsrekt.com/
- Per-post detail: https://thatsrekt.com/post/{chainSlug}/{onchainId} (e.g. `/post/base/1`)
- About + how it works: https://thatsrekt.com/about
- Active posters list: https://thatsrekt.com/posters
- Docs (architecture, integration guide, deployments table): https://thatsrekt.com/docs
- Public GraphQL endpoint: https://thatsrekt.com/graphql
- Telegram alerts channel: https://t.me/thatsrekt_alerts
- Project X / Twitter: https://x.com/ThatsRekt_
- Source code: https://github.com/JeronimoHoulin/thatsRekt

## On-chain registry contracts

| Chain | Proxy address | Status |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Base mainnet (chainId 8453) | `0x390f7b37545CaD278dD3DADC92a20b9f45865936` | live (canonical) |
| Optimism mainnet (chainId 10) | `0x75bDe0394Dd0D92a2cEd1E0E4Fd5abB21319fD0e` | interim test |

Other chains (Ethereum, Arbitrum, Polygon, BSC, Blast, Avalanche) are planned but not yet deployed.

## Data model summary

Each post has:
- `id` — composite `{chainSlug}-{onchainId}` (e.g. `base-1`)
- `poster` — address of the on-chain author (must be on the chain's whitelist when calling `post(...)`)
- `title` — required, ≤ 200 bytes UTF-8, the headline
- `attackers` — array of addresses suspected of perpetrating the attack
- `victims` — array of victim contract addresses
- `note` — free-form Markdown body, optional
- `attackedAt` — unix timestamp the attack occurred (≤ block.timestamp)
- `confirmations` / `disconfirmations` — counts of whitelisted on-chain ✓ / ✗ votes
- `netScore` — `confirmations - disconfirmations`

## Reading the registry on-chain

Two stable views integrators rely on, both pure and gas-free:
- `attackerScore(address)` — accumulated reputation score for an address (sum of net-positive confirmations across posts where it appears as an attacker)
- `isVictim(address)` — boolean, true if the address is currently listed as a victim on at least one active post

These are the integration surface for wallets / DEXes / lending markets who want to gate or warn on suspicious counterparties without having to index the registry themselves.

## How alerts flow

1. A whitelisted security team spots a live exploit.
2. They sign a `post(...)` tx from a whitelisted address.
3. The indexer picks it up (~5s).
4. The web feed updates and the Telegram bot broadcasts (~10s after that).
5. Other whitelisters call `confirm(...)` or `disconfirm(...)` to vouch.
6. Wallets / protocols reading `attackerScore(...)` and `isVictim(...)` see the new state.

End-to-end latency from chain inclusion to Telegram message is typically 5–15 seconds.
